key_insight: Disordered Se/S substitution in 2H-NbSeS suppresses CDW order and reveals superconductivity best explained by two distinct nodeless bands rather than a single anisotropic gap.
engineering_relevance: The work clarifies how chemical disorder can separate CDW effects from intrinsic multiband pairing, informing the design and interpretation of layered superconductors with tunable anisotropy and critical fields.
method: Grow single-crystalline 2H-NbSeS with random Se/S substitution
Measure upper and lower critical fields, superfluid density, and electronic specific heat
Compare single-band and dirty-limit two-band models
evidence_signal: The upper critical field shows strong upward curvature and fits a dirty-limit two-band model, while thermodynamic and magnetic measurements support two fully gapped superconducting gaps.
watch_item: Because disorder alters band scattering and can mask intrinsic electronic structure, the two-gap interpretation should be checked against complementary spectroscopic probes.
